elements: World coordinate axes (Xw: red arrow, Yw: green arrow, Zw: blue arrow), with bold axes labels
Point P (black sphere, size increased for visibility) and clear bold label 'P' or 'P?' as appropriate
Origin of world coordinate system marked with 'O'
Ground plane grid for reference (subtle, not distracting)
Horizon line labeled 'h' (thick solid black, prominent label)
Vanishing points v1 (blue), v2 (green), v3 (red)—large contrasting spheres with distinct color and bold labels
Vertical plane (semi-opaque gray, slab-like, passes through G and P) with increased thickness and opacity for prominence
Construction lines: l1, l2, l3, l4, each with thick/dash styles and bold, enlarged labels
Dashed projection and reference lines (correct thickness/dash to match figure, semi-transparent, labeled appropriately)
Point G (gold sphere, bold label 'G')—projection of P to ground plane
Intersection points on axes labeled Px, Py, Pz (distinct shapes/colors, bold, with on-figure mini-legend as overlay)
On-figure legend overlay: graphical key explaining all special points (P, G, Px, Py, Pz), possibly as a small side panel or hover/clickable popup

interactions.label: Only axes and unknown P?
interactions.teaches: Switches between the minimal ambiguous configuration (part a) with only coordinate axes and unknown P, and the full scene with context and construction, highlighting the importance of scene information.

interactions.label: Show construction lines
interactions.teaches: Toggles visibility of all construction lines, vanishing points, the vertical plane, and their labels to clarify or simplify the geometric reasoning for finding 3D coordinates.

interactions.label: Move point P vertically
interactions.teaches: Lets you change P's actual height above the ground, showing how the Yw coordinate is constructed from the ground reference.

interactions.label: Move ground projection G (Xw)
interactions.teaches: Moves the ground contact point G along the Xw axis, demonstrating how its position defines the X/Z world coordinates for P.

demo_steps.title: Ambiguous P: No Context
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with the bare essentials: only the 3D axes and an unknown point 'P?'. You see there's no way to deduce where P? truly lies in the 3D space—countless possibilities exist along the visual ray. This mirrors the top panel of the figure and highlights why context is crucial.

demo_steps.title: Add Context: Projecting G
demo_steps.narration: Now we've switched on the full geometry—including the ground projection G, the vertical slab, and all the construction lines. Once you know P belongs to an object whose base touches the ground at G, the Xw and Zw coordinates of P become determined by G. Colorful vanishing points and bold construction lines show exactly how these coordinates are found.

demo_steps.title: Build Height: Yw from L3, L4
demo_steps.narration: Drag P upwards: as P's vertical position changes, the construction lines l3 and l4 project from the vanishing point on the horizon to P and then to the Yw axis, locking in Py—its height. The vertical slab visibly thickens this relationship. As construction lines update, so do the labels and legend for Px, Py, and Pz, showing exactly where all 3D coordinates appear.

demo_steps.title: Move G: Change Xw/Zw
demo_steps.narration: Now let's shift G side-to-side. As you slide G along the Xw axis, watch how P (and its projections Px and Pz) follow—the construction lines adjust to reflect new Xw/Zw values for P. This demonstrates how knowing just the right bit of scene context (ground contact G) unlocks the full 3D location from a single image.

plan.camera_suggestion: Start with a 3/4 perspective view, slightly above the ground plane and offset to reveal world axes, all vanishing points, the horizon, and the full height of P. Ensure all labeled points and the vertical plane are clearly visible. Zoom so that any movement of P or G stays entirely within view, with extra margin for the legend overlay.
plan.notes: All line and point labels (l1–l4, P, G, Px, Py, Pz, vanishing points) should use bold, enlarged, and high-contrast text. Construction lines must be appropriately thick or dashed for clarity. The vertical plane should have higher opacity (e.g., 0.5–0.7), and not occlude axis labels. The on-figure legend can be an overlay or a hover/click popup for accessibility. Vanishing points should be easily seen even from a distance. Ensure layering prevents visual ambiguity: axes and critical points are always on top of the plane.

plan.contextChunk:
![Calibrated world axes. Each axis is calibrated using the measurement of a scene element parallel to the world-coordinate axis. In this example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the table base (50 cm). From the calibrated axes, you can see that the office is around 250 cm wide.](figures/single_view_3d/office_all_calibrated_box.png){width="100%" #fig-office_all_calibrated_box}

From this calibrated system we can directly read the height of the
camera. The location of the camera coincides with the location of the
horizon line because, in this example, the ground plane is horizontal.
It seems that the center of the camera is approximately 163 cm above the
ground.

There are many ways in which we can arrive to the same result.

### Locating a 3D Point

In the absence of any other information, we can not recover the 3D
location of a single point $\mathbf{P}$ from a single view as
illustrated in @fig-locating_a_3d_point \[a\]. But real world scenes are
composed by a multitude of objects providing context, which allows
solving the 3D estimation problem even with just a single image. In the
previous section we showed how to estimate the height of an object, here
we show how to estimated the 3D coordinates of a point.

The basic procedure is illustrated in @fig-locating_a_3d_point \[b\]. The
trick consists in using context (what object is the point part of, where
is the horizon line of the scene and where are the vanishing points) to
estimate 3D from a single image. It suffices to know that the point
$\mathbf{P}$ belongs to an object that is in contact with the ground and
the geometry of this object provides the information to localize the
projection of the point $\mathbf{P}$ on the ground plane, which we
denote with $\mathbf{G}$.

![Figure (a) In the absence of any other information, we can not recover the 3D location of point $\mathbf{P}$. (b) If we are given a way to project the point P into the ground, then we can read out the coordinates of the point by projecting into the world axis.](figures/single_view_3d/locating_a_3d_point.png){width="100%" #fig-locating_a_3d_point}

Once we know the image location of the projection of the 3D point
$\mathbf{P}$ to the ground, $\mathbf{G}$, we can project this point to
the world-coordinate axis. To estimate the $Y$-coordinate we use a
similar method as the one described in the previous section. We first
find the line, $\boldsymbol{l}_3$, that passes by the point $\mathbf{G}$
and the origin, and then find the intersection with the horizon line,
giving the point $\mathbf{v}$. Next, find the line, $\boldsymbol{l}_4$,
that passes by $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{P}$. The intersection of
$\boldsymbol{l}_4$ with the $Y_w$-axis gives the coordinate $P_y$. The
$P_x$ and $P_z$ coordinates can be obtained by connecting $\mathbf{G}$
with the vanishing points $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$.

Here we have been using the ground plane as a reference. But the same
idea can be applied if we know the projection of the point $\mathbf{P}$

elements: World coordinate axes (Xw, Yw, Zw) with colored arrows
Black point labeled P (arbitrary 3D point)
Ground plane (as implicit reference)
Origin of world coordinate system
Horizon line labeled 'h'
Three vanishing points (v1: blue, v2: green, v3: red)
Dashed projection lines between: origin to v1, origin to v2, origin to horizon (v), G to origin, G to vanishing points, v to P, P to axes
Intersection points labeled Px (on Xw), Py (on Yw), Pz (on Zw)
Point G, projection of P onto ground plane
Gray vertical plane through G and P
Labels for all lines (l1, l2, l3, l4), and coordinates

interactions.label: Move point P vertically
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how changing the actual height of point P affects the construction and its location along the world Yw axis.

interactions.label: Show construction lines
interactions.teaches: Lets you inspect how lines and vanishing points are used to recover each 3D coordinate by toggling their visibility.

interactions.label: Move ground point G (Xw)
interactions.teaches: Shows how the position of the object's ground contact, G, sets the X and Z coordinates of point P by projection.

demo_steps.title: The Underdetermined Point P
demo_steps.narration: Here you see only the coordinate axes and an unknown point P. With just this, its exact location in 3D space cannot be determined. This is the fundamental ambiguity of single-view geometry: a single point has infinite possible world positions without context.

demo_steps.title: Adding Ground Projection G
demo_steps.narration: Now, we reveal point G, the shadow or projection of P onto the ground plane. This one extra piece of context means we can align G with the Xw and Zw axes. Dashed lines show how G connects to the vanishing points and the origin, letting us lock in the X and Z values for P.

demo_steps.title: Recovering Full 3D Coordinates
demo_steps.narration: With G in place, we project up from G to P and now use the construction lines to pinpoint P's height on the Yw axis. The lines l3 and l4 trace through the vanishing point on the horizon to P and then vertically to the Yw axis, giving us Py. Drag the controls to see how P's full 3D position changes in the calibrated world.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Start with an off-axis, perspective 3/4 view that shows the Xw, Yw, Zw axes clearly and allows the vertical dimension to be easily seen. Zoom so that both point P and all three vanishing points fit comfortably in the frame, along with the horizon.
interactionPlan.notes: Dashed construction lines should be semi-transparent and disappear smoothly when toggled off. Labels should remain legible from multiple camera angles. The gray vertical plane through G and P should have transparency so it does not obscure the axes or lines. Emphasize depth layering by using subtle shading or drop-shadow effects.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original left figure (a) is not represented at all in the generated figure.
The question-marked label 'P?' from (a) is not present.
The labeling and coloring for vanishing points and construction lines mostly match, but label font sizes and line thicknesses deviate.
Figure (b)'s thick slab for the vertical plane is thinner and more subtle in the generated figure.
Some labels (e.g., l1, l2, l3, l4) are de-emphasized and less readable than in the source.
evaluation.notes: Highly interactive, captures most of the 3D construction well, but omits the (a) half of the source figure and minor labeling/visual issues persist.
action_items: Add a toggle or step to display the minimal axes and labeled 'P?' configuration from part (a) of the source figure.
Bolden and resize labels for l1, l2, l3, l4 and ensure all construction lines have correct thickness and dash style to improve visual fidelity.
Increase the opacity or thickness of the vertical plane to better match the prominent slab in the source.
Slightly enlarge vanishing point markers and clarify their color contrast for better legibility.
Consider providing an on-figure legend or explanation for the points (e.g., P, G, Px, Py, Pz) for added pedagogical clarity.

feedback.rationale: The omission of the entire left figure (a) and its key labeled primitive ('P?') points to an incomplete plan that didn't capture all necessary conceptual components from the source.
action_items: Add a toggle or step to display the minimal axes and labeled 'P?' configuration from part (a) of the source figure.

Increase the opacity or thickness of the vertical plane to better match the prominent slab in the source.
Slightly enlarge vanishing point markers and clarify their color contrast for better legibility.
Consider providing an on-figure legend or explanation for the points (e.g., P, G, Px, Py, Pz) for added pedagogical clarity.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The vanishing points v1, v2, and v3 are not color-coded (blue, green, red) as in the source image and are all black.
The ground projections and vertical construction marks (Pz, Px, Py) in the generated figure use black disks rather than thick bars as in the source image.
The angled black tick marks on the axes in panel (b) are present, but the thickness and alignment do not closely match the strong visual style of the source.
Some dashed lines and planes are visually less bold/distinct than in the original, especially the gray vertical rectangle, which lacks the dark outline and solid fill.
The color of the Z_w axis in both panels is green in the source but appears lighter in the generated, and the axes arrowheads are less pronounced.
evaluation.notes: Highly interactive and well-aligned with the original, but some visual style details—color coding, boldness, and tick/bar marks—are not fully faithful.
action_items: Color vanishing points v1 (blue), v2 (green), v3 (red) in panel (b) to match the original exactly.
Improve vertical and axis tick/bar marks to use thick rectangular bars rather than disks for Px, Py, and Pz.
Enhance the visual ‘weight’ of dashed lines and gray planes to better match the boldness of the source illustration.
Adjust axis arrowhead size and color saturation for better visual parity with the source.
Review all label placements and font sizes for even closer alignment with the source figure, especially overlapping or tight label placements.

feedback.rationale: There are no conceptual or interaction failures, only minor visual and styling discrepancies that do not merit further iteration given the high scores and overall faithful execution.
